column2: Set interface name without msg in ROS2  When creating custom message types in ROS1 (noetic), I can store the *.msg files in a subfolder named msg. In the CMake file, I just type the name of the file without msg/ in add_message_files(...). The resulting message type is named as <pkg_name>/<file-name (without .msg)>  When creating interfaces in ROS2 (foxy), I have to type the file name in my CMake file including the relative path in rosidl_generate_interfaces(...) like msg/<filename.msg>. The resulting interface name also includes msg: <pkg_name>/msg/<file-name (without .msg)>, but actually I don't want the exact path as an interface name.  So my question is: Is it possible to store the files in the msg folder for a better folder structure but set the name of the resulting interface differently? Set interface name without msg in ROS2  When creating custom message types in ROS1 (noetic), I can store the *.msg files in a subfolder named msg. In the CMake file, I just type the name of the file without msg/ in add_message_files(...). The resulting message type is named as <pkg_name>/<file-name (without .msg)>  When creating interfaces in ROS2 (foxy), I have to type the file name in my CMake file including the relative path in rosidl_generate_interfaces(...) like msg/<filename.msg>. The resulting interface name also includes msg: <pkg_name>/msg/<file-name (without .msg)>, but actually I don't want the exact path as an interface name.  So my question is: Is it possible to store the files in the msg folder for a better folder structure but set the name of the resulting interface differently? In ROS1, message types were referred to by package and type name, like std_msgs/String, without the folder prefix. In ROS2, the naming convention is stricter: generated interface types include the folder in their type names (e.g., <pkg_name>/msg/<MessageType>). There is currently no supported way to change the resulting interface name to remove the 'msg' segment if you store your message files in the msg directory—the generated name will always include the interface directory by design for clarity and consistency in ROS2. If you want a different name, you would need to place your .

I am using the rospy with numpy tutorial to create a publisher and subscriber that send numpy arrays to listener that uses an ApproximateTimeSynchronizer. However, I receive an error when I try to instantiate the listener:  [WARN] [1693265081.824595]: Cannot use message filters with non-stamped messages. Use the 'allow_headerless' constructor option to auto-assign ROS time to headerless messages. So my understanding is that my message must contain a header with a time stamp. Is it possible to add a header with a timestamp to a numpy_msg? And if so, how do I this?  My message publisher construction:  from rospy_tutorials.msg import Floats from rospy.numpy_msg import numpy_msg  l_lines_pub = rospy.Publisher('/l_lines_output', numpy_msg(Floats), queue_size=10) r_lines_pub = rospy.Publisher('/r_lines_output', numpy_msg(Floats), queue_size=10)  l_lines_msg = np.asarray(ARRAY1) r_lines_msg = np.asarray(ARRAY2)  l_lines_pub.publish(l_lines_msg) r_lines_pub.publish(r_lines_msg) And my subscriber looks like:  l_lines_sub = Subscriber('/l_lines_output', numpy_msg(Floats)) r_lines_sub = Subscriber('/r_lines_output', numpy_msg(Floats))  ats = ApproximateTimeSynchronizer([l_lines_sub, r_lines_sub], queue_size=5, slop=0.015) ats.registerCallback(gotData) Thanks! Python dependencies with 'sudo pip' (as root) can cause issues because it installs packages globally and may interfere with system-managed Python packages. ROS2's rosdep installs dependencies system-wide, which often means using 'sudo pip'. This may cause conflicts and missing modules like 'hid' because some dependencies may end up in different locations or user environments. The workaround is to use a Python virtual environment for your ROS2 workspace and install dependencies there. You can tell rosdep to use the virtual environment's pip by not running it with sudo and making sure your virtual environment is active when running 'rosdep

Thank you! For holonomic drivetrains with non-zero starting velocities, commonly used path and trajectory planning algorithms include sampling-based planners like RRT* (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree Star) and PRM (Probabilistic Roadmap), often paired with trajectory optimization or smoothing methods (such as time-parameterized splines or polynomial trajectory generators) that can account for initial velocities. To avoid collisions while respecting a non-zero starting velocity, you should: (1) generate a collision-free path using the sampling-based planner, (2) use trajectory generation techniques (like time-parameterized cubic or quintic

For an Ackerman steering robot using ROS Navigation2, the recommended placement of the base_link is generally at a central, symmetrical point on the robot—ideally at the midpoint of the rear axle (between the two drive wheels). This matches the location used by most motion models, including Reeds-Shepp, and is also the conventional location for 'base_link' in ROS. The x-axis should point forward, y-axis to the left, and z-axis up. All position and sensor transforms should be set relative to this base_link. To compare trajectories from your recorded odometry topics in ROS2, including quantitatively evaluating metrics like distance error, you can use the evo tool (https://github.com/MichaelGrupp/evo). evo is widely used for trajectory evaluation and can compute differences, translation/rotation errors, etc., between trajectories. You can convert your ROS2 bag topics (e.g., /odom_local, /odom_global, /odom_ackermann) to TUM or KITTI format using available scripts or message converters, then use evo to generate plots and compute statistics. Is there any equivalent in to that in Gazebo Ignition? To spawn robots programmatically in Gazebo Ignition using ROS 2, you use the service interface provided by the `ros_ign_gazebo` package. This is similar to the Gazebo Classic `spawn_entity` launch file from the `gazebo_ros` package. In Ignition, you typically call the `/spawn_entity` ROS 2 service from the `ros_ign_gazebo` package to spawn robot models into the simulation.

Trouble Installing ROS 2 Humble on Ubuntu 20.04 with arm64/x86_64 Architecture  I've been encountering an issue while attempting to install ROS 2 Humble on my Ubuntu 20.04 system. My machine supports both arm64 and x86_64 architectures. Despite following the official installation instructions closely, I run into the "unable to locate package ros-humble-desktop" error every time I try to execute the installation command.  Here are the steps I've followed based on the instructions from the ROS.org page for Humble:  1. Added the ROS 2 repository and keyring as follows:  sudo apt update && sudo apt install curl -y  sudo curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ros/rosdistro/master/ros.key -o /usr/share/keyrings/ros-archive-keyring.gpg  echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/ros-archive-keyring.gpg] http://packages.ros.org/ros2/ubuntu $(. /etc/os-release && echo $UBUNTU_CODENAME) main" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/ros2.list > /dev/null 2. Updated and upgraded my package lists:     sudo apt update    sudo apt upgrade 3. Attempted to install ROS 2 Humble packages:     sudo apt install ros-humble-desktop However, this results in the "unable to locate package ros-humble-desktop" error.  Interestingly, I came across a similar issue on ROS Answers where a user encountered package location errors due to their system running on an ARMhf platform. However, after checking my system using dpkg --print-architecture and lscpu, I confirmed that my system is on arm64 and x86_64, which should be compatible with ROS 2 Humble according to REP 2000.  I'm reaching out to see if anyone has faced a similar issue or could offer guidance on resolving this error. Could this problem be related to my prior attempts to uninstall ROS 2 Foxy and clean my system? Or is there something else I'm missing in the setup process for ROS 2 Humble on Ubuntu 20.04?  Any advice or suggestions would be greatly appreciated. Thank you in advance for your help! First, ensure both detection nodes are publishing their outputs with proper timestamps. You can use message_filters (such as ApproximateTimeSynchronizer) to synchronize the messages in your fusion node. Next, you need to register or calibrate the coordinate frames of both sensors (lidar and camera) using their extrinsic parameters and use tf2 for spatial alignment. The 'capabilities' error when launching MoveIt2 configurations usually indicates that there's a missing or misconfigured 'capabilities' entry in one of your configuration or launch files. This might be due to changes in MoveIt2's API or configuration structure between versions. Check your MoveIt2 configuration files (YAML and launch files) for a 'capabilities' field—if it exists, try removing it or updating it according to the MoveIt2 documentation for ROS2 Humble. Also, make sure you are using the correct versions of MoveIt2 and your configuration files are compatible with ROS2

i < info_.joints.size(); i++)   {     state_interfaces.emplace_back(hardware_interface::StateInterface(       info_.joints[i].name,      // joint name from controller.yaml       "my_state_handle",         // a made up name       &hw_states_[i]             // information for controlling the joint     ));   }    return states_interfaces; } This didn't work because there is no matching function for the call hardware_interface::StateInterface(const std::string &name, const std::string &interface_name, MyStateHandle *value_ptr=nullptr) because there is only the function hardware_interface::StateInterface(const std::string &name, const std::string &interface_name, double *value_ptr=nullptr)  I want to know if there is a way to pass a class full of control data from the hardware interface to the controller, or am I on a completely wrong track? In ROS 2's hardware_interface, StateInterface is specifically designed to pass pointers to double values, which is why the constructor only accepts double* and not custom classes. This means you cannot directly pass an entire class with multiple data fields (such as your MyStateHandle) through the StateInterface as you are proposing. The recommended approach is to expose each element of your joint state (e.g., position, velocity, effort) as individual StateInterface objects, each pointing to a double within your storage structure. If you need to associate more complex data, you should handle that association internally within your hardware class or compose

The Octomap and the projected Octomap are shown below.  Is there any way to change the direction from which Octomap creates the projection? Octomap and Projected Octomap not lining up  I am trying to use a projected Octomap as an occupancy grid to pass onto the ROS navigation stack, but when I create one using the octomap_server package, it creates an occupancy grid which is rotated 90 degrees from the top down view (i.e., It projects from the side when it should be projecting downwards). The Octomap and the projected Octomap are shown below.  Is there any way to change the direction from which Octomap creates the projection? Octomap and Projected Octomap not lining up  I am trying to use a projected Octomap as an occupancy grid to pass onto the ROS navigation stack, but when I create one using the octomap_server package, it creates an occupancy grid which is rotated 90 degrees from the top down view (i.e., It projects from the side when it should be projecting downwards). The Octomap and the projected Octomap are shown below.  Is there any way to change the direction from which Octomap creates the projection? Yes, you can change the projection direction in the octomap_server. By default, the projection used to create the 2D occupancy grid (`projected_map`) is along the Z axis (i.e., projecting downwards). If you see it projecting from the side (e.g., along X or Y), it's likely your Octomap's frame is misaligned or the octomap_server node's `frame_id` parameter is not set correctly. Make sure your Octomap data and the projection frame are in the same coordinate system, with Z pointing upwards. You might need to set the correct `frame_id`

Oriented Bounding Box in RVIZ  I want to visualize an OrientedBoundingBox from Open3D on RVIZ, I have seen the :  RvizVisualTools::publishWireframeRectangle RVIZ Line Segments publishWireframeRectangle has two dimensions width, and height, while I still have length, and for RVIZ Line Segment I have to develop the function myself, I wonder if there is a ready to use function to publish RVIZ Marker for OrientedBoundingBox which is ready to use, can you please tell me if there is any? thanks in advance. There is not a built-in or ready-to-use function in RViz or the standard ROS visualization packages to directly display an OrientedBoundingBox (OBB) as provided by Open3D. Typically, you would need to create a custom Marker of type 'LINE_LIST' or 'CUBE' with a pose (position and orientation) matching your OBB. Most existing helper functions like publishWireframeRectangle only handle axis-aligned shapes or provide limited configurability. So, to visualize an arbitrary OBB, you will usually have to manually publish a custom Marker message (with the appropriate pose and dimensions

I am working with a gazebo garden setup where I use a gz-ros2 and ros2-ros1 bridge in order to work with ros as well (I know this is not a good setup and the endeavor is to only use ros2 at some point.) To publish the transform of elements (such as an RGBD camera) in a Gazebo Garden simulation, you typically need to create and publish TF information so that ROS tools can visualize it. If you are using the Ignition-Gazebo-ROS2 bridge (gz-ros2), you can achieve this by using the 'ros_gz_bridge' package to relay Gazebo topics (including pose information) to ROS2. Within ROS2, use the 'tf2_ros' package and a node (e.g., a custom node or the robot_state_publisher if you have a URDF)

How to use Planner Selector in the Nav2 BT's xml file  I want to use the PlannerSelector action in Nav2 in order to use multiple planners and choose which one to use in the runtime. I modified the default behavior tree xml a little bit in order to do this, and I added a PlannerSelector BT node in my xml file as you can see in the xml file below.  The main issue is that the robot always uses the default planner. I tried to change the planner by publishing the string message "GridBased1" in the topic /planner_selector (ros2 topic pub /planner_selector std_msgs/msg/String "data: GridBased1") but the robot was still using the default planner. If I change the default planner before starting the robot and execute the process again then the planner normally changes.  To sum up, it seems that the different planners work normally when assigning them as default_planner in the PlannerSelector but the system always uses the default planner and it cannot be changed by publishing in the /planner_selector topic.  Below you can see the parameters related to planner_server:  planner_server:  ros__parameters:   expected_planner_frequency: 20.0   use_sim_time: True   planner_plugins: ["GridBased", "GridBased1"]   GridBased:      plugin: "nav2_navfn_planner/NavfnPlanner"     tolerance: 0.5     use_astar: false     allow_unknown: true    GridBased1:     plugin: "nav2_straightline_planner/StraightLine"     interpolation_resolution: 0.1 Below you can see the BT's xml file that I use:  <root main_tree_to_execute="MainTree">  <BehaviorTree ID="MainTree">   <RecoveryNode number_of_retries="6" name="NavigateRecovery">    <PipelineSequence name="NavigateWithReplanning">     <RateController hz="1.0">       <PipelineSequence name="SelectPlanner">         <PlannerSelector selected_planner="{selected_planner}" default_planner="GridBased" topic_name="planner_selector"/>         <RecoveryNode number_of_retries="1" name="ComputePathToPose">           <ComputePathToPose goal="{goal}" path="{path}" planner_id="{selected_planner}"/>           <ReactiveFallback name="ComputePathToPoseRecoveryFallback">             <GoalUpdated/>             <ClearEntireCostmap name="ClearGlobalCostmap-Context" service_name="global_costmap/clear_entirely_global_costmap"/>           </ReactiveFallback>         </RecoveryNode>       </PipelineSequence>     </RateController>     <RecoveryNode number_of_retries="1" name="FollowPath">       <FollowPath path="{path}" controller_id="FollowPath"/>       <ReactiveFallback name="FollowPathRecoveryFallback">         <GoalUpdated/>         <ClearEntireCostmap name="ClearLocalCostmap-Context" service_name="local_costmap/clear_entirely_local_costmap"/>       </ReactiveFallback>     </RecoveryNode>   </PipelineSequence>   <ReactiveFallback name="RecoveryFallback">     <GoalUpdated/>     <RoundRobin name="RecoveryActions">       <Sequence name="ClearingActions">         <ClearEntireCostmap name="ClearLocalCostmap-Subtree" service_name="local_costmap/clear_entirely_local_costmap"/>         <ClearEntireCostmap name="ClearGlobalCostmap-Subtree" service_name="global_costmap/clear_entirely_global_costmap"/>       </Sequence>       <Spin spin_dist="1.57"/>       <Wait wait_duration="5"/>       <BackUp backup_dist="0.15" backup_speed="0.025"/>     </RoundRobin>   </ReactiveFallback>  </RecoveryNode> I am using:  turtlebot3 package for the simulation with the default launchers for the gazebo and navigation packages ROS2 galactic Ubuntu 20.04 If you have any workable BT xml file with a PlannerSelector feel free to share. Implementing a prismatic joint within a joint trajectory controller  I'm wondering if someone has some ideas about how to make a prismatic axis work with ros2 controllers.  I am using a UR10e which is mountd on an igus drylin ZLW 7th Axis controlled by Dryve D1. I have an API for it to communicate with the Axis to initialize, run and control it. It has an encoder so after homing I can extrapolate the exact position, velocity and other info. The official nav2 stack does not include TEB as a local planner option in Humble; only planners like DWB, MPPI, Regulated Pure Pursuit, and Rotation Shim Controllers are supported. The teb_local_planner repository does not have a Humble branch, and there is no ROS2-compliant release for Humble. I would like to test my robot's ArUco recognition and processing abilities by putting some markers around environments in classic Gazebo.  All the tutorials I see online seem to be for older versions of Gazebo and (frankly) use some very elaborate tricks.  What is the canonical way of adding a marker to Gazebo?  EDIT: My version of Gazebo is 11.10.2, by "older versions of Gazebo" I mean older versions of Gazebo classic specifically. Sorry for any confusion. To put ArUco markers in Gazebo Classic 11.10.2, generate ArUco marker images (e.g., using the OpenCV library) and use them as textures on plane or box models in Gazebo. You can create a new Gazebo model (e.g., a simple box or flat plane) and apply the marker image as a material/texture in the model.sdf file. Place these models in your environment wherever needed. This approach is the canonical way to simulate visual markers in Gazebo Classic for vision-based robot tasks.

In ROS, is it possible to do dynamic reconfigure with "nested" parameters?  Not positive I have the terminology right, but the idea is that I have a ROS 1 node that can have multiple "layers" of namespaces for some of the parameters. So the parameter list is something like:  /perception_node/map_size /perception_node/sensor_1/max_range /perception_node/sensor_2/max_range It doesn't have to be set up this way, the key here is that there are multiple "sensor" objects that have the same parameter names, but can have different values. And the sensor names themselves should ideally be configurable from the launch file or a YAML file.  I'm able to get it up and running so that it all initializes correctly, I can have a sensor struct like this:  struct Sensor {     string sensor_name;  // < This gets set in the constructor     float max_range;      void configure(ros::NodeHandle & nodeHandle)     {         max_range = nodeHandle.param(sensor_name + "/max_range");     } }; And that works, but I'd really like to use dynamic reconfigure with this setup too. I've used dynamic reconfigure before on other projects, so I know the basics of creating a ".cfg" file and everything.  But dynamic reconfigure doesn't seem to like parameter names with "/" symbols in them, at least when I tried the following it failed to compile:  gen = ParameterGenerator()  gen.add("sensor_1/max_range", float_t ...etc.... My initial idea was that since the ".cfg" file is (I think) really a python file, I could do something clever here and read the sensor names from a configuration file rather than hard coding it like in the above example.  I also tried using groups, but it looks like you're not allowed to use the same parameter name more than once even if you do this:  sensor_1_params = gen.addGroup("sensor_1") sensor_1_params.add("max_range", float_t ...etc...  sensor_2_params = gen.addGroup("sensor_2") sensor_2_params.add("max_range", float_t ...etc... #Nope! This fails to compile too The only thing I can think of right now is to get rid of the slashes, so that the parameters are called something like "sensor_1_max_range" and "sensor_2_max_range" and so on. The issue being that it would make the config callback kind of ugly, because on the C++ side I'd have to do something like:  if (sensor_name == "sensor_1") {     max_range = config.sensor_1_max_range; } else if (sensor_name == "sensor_2") {     max_range = config.sensor_2_max_range; } // And many more lines... I also feel like this kind of defeats the purpose of having the sensor names in a configuration file, because now any time we want to add or remove sensors we need to edit the configuration file AND the C++ code.  So, I guess I'm wondering is it possible to actually do something like what I want? Have people had to deal with this before? Or am I better off restructuring things? Configuration wrt. automatic chaining of `setup.bash` prefix paths from `colcon build`  I recently found (to my great surprise) that upon issuing 'source install/setup.bash' for my ROS 2 install (built from source), that both Gazebo Sim and ros_gz_bridge were also sourced automatically.  So I had a look at the ROS 2 install/setup.bash script, and indeed it mentions:  # This script extends the environment with the environment of other prefix # paths which were sourced when this file was generated as well as all packages # contained in this prefix path. So probably I did the following:  Compile and source ROS 2, Compile and source Gazebo Sim, Compile and source ros_gz_bridge, Compile ROS 2 again. I wonder if many people are aware of that behavior. In any case I did not know of this automatic chaining, nor was expecting it, as:  In above case, it is obviously undesirable that the ROS 2 setup.bash script would chain the Gazebo and ros_gz_bridge scripts,  But even in other cases this can be undesired. E.g. the ros_gz_bridge obviously depends on ROS 2 and Gazebo Sim, but even then I might want to source a different ROS 2 install (e.g. with some local changes, irrelevant to ros_gz_bridge). So I don't necessarily want the ros_gz_bridge to automatically source the ROS 2 install that was sourced at build time.  Is there some documentation wrt. this behavior? So I had a look at the ROS 2 install/setup.bash script, and indeed it mentions:  # This script extends the environment with the environment of other prefix # paths which were sourced when this file was generated as well as all packages # contained in this prefix path. So probably I did the following:  Compile and source ROS 2, Compile and source Gazebo Sim, Compile and source ros_gz_bridge, Compile ROS 2 again. I wonder if many people are aware of that behavior. In any case I did not know of this automatic chaining, nor was expecting it, as:  In above case, it is obviously undesirable that the ROS 2 setup.bash script would chain the Gazebo and ros_gz_bridge scripts,  But even in other cases this can be undesired. E.g. the ros_gz_bridge obviously depends on ROS 2 and Gazebo Sim, but even then I might want to source a different ROS 2 install (e.g. with some local changes, irrelevant to ros_gz_bridge). So I don't necessarily want the ros_gz_bridge to automatically source the ROS 2 install that was sourced at build time.  Is there some documentation wrt. this behavior? There is some documentation about this behavior in the official ROS 2 colcon and workspace tutorials. Essentially, when you build a workspace with colcon and source `install/setup.bash`, it not only sets up the packages from that workspace but also chains (sources) any environment that was already active when the build happened. This means if you previously sourced another workspace or package (like Gazebo Sim or ros_gz_bridge), those prefixes get embedded in the new setup scripts, causing them to be automatically sourced when you later source the new workspace's setup.bash. This behavior is explained in the colcon

Can't find Apply Force Torque plugin in gazebo garden  Environment: OS: Linux Ubuntu 22.04 gazebo version: garden  Problem: When I‘m following the tutorial here, I can't find Apply Force Torque plugin Does my gazebo need update or there's something wrong? To check if the Apply Force Torque plugin is available in Gazebo Garden, first ensure you have installed all gazebo-garden packages and plugins. In Garden, some plugins that were provided by default in previous versions may need to be installed or enabled separately. You can list available plugins using the command `gz plugin --list-model`. If the plugin is missing, make sure your installation is up-to-date by running `sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade`, then reinstall the `gazebo-garden` plugins package.
